}}
The '''Zambia national football team''' represents [[Zambia]] in men's [[association football]] and is governed by the [[Football Association of Zambia]] (FAZ). During the 1980s, they were known as the '''KK 11''', after founding president Dr. [[Kenneth Kaunda]] ("KK") who ruled Zambia from 1964 to 1991. After the country adopted [[Politics of Zambia#1991: Move to a multiparty state|multiparty politics]], the side was nicknamed ''Chipolopolo'', which means the "Copper Bullets".<ref name="FAZ history">{{cite web | url=http://www.fazfootball.com/content/history-of-zambia-national-team | title=History of Zambian National Team | website=fazfootball.com | publisher=Football Association of Zambia | access-date=29 October 2016 | archive-date=24 March 2019 | archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190324233326/http://www.fazfootball.com/content/history-of-zambia-national-team | url-status=usurped }}</ref> The team won an [[Africa Cup of Nations]] title in [[2012 Africa Cup of Nations|2012]]. The team has also become the most successful team in the [[COSAFA Cup]], surpassing [[Zimbabwe national football team|Zimbabwe]] after winning the [[2023 COSAFA Cup|2023 edition]].

==History==
===Zambian Air Force Flight 319===
{{main|1993 Zambia national football team air disaster}}

Tragedy struck the Zambian team when the military plane (REG: AF-319) transporting the team to Senegal for a 1994 FIFA World Cup qualifier crashed late in the evening of April 27, 1993. Three stops were planned for re-fuelling, but at the first stop, in Brazzaville, engine problems were noted on the [[de Havilland Canada DHC-5 Buffalo|Buffalo DHC-5D]] of the [[Zambia Air Force]]. Despite this, the flight continued, and a few minutes after take-off from Libreville, Gabon, where the second stopover had taken place, one of the engines caught fire and stopped. The pilot, who had already made a flight from Mauritius the day before, accidentally shut down the other engine, which was still running. The loss of power, during the climb after take-off, caused the plane to fall and crash into the water 500m off the coast. All 30 passengers and crew, including 18 players, were killed in the accident.<ref>{{cite magazine |url=http://fourfourtwo.com/blogs/africacupofnations2012/archive/2012/02/12/zambia-s-remarkable-journey-makes-them-winners-regardless.aspx |title=Zambia's remarkable journey makes them winners regardless |magazine=FourFourTwo |date=12 February 2012 |access-date=2 December 2013 |archive-date=28 June 2012 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120628045205/http://fourfourtwo.com/blogs/africacupofnations2012/archive/2012/02/12/zambia-s-remarkable-journey-makes-them-winners-regardless.aspx |url-status=live }}</ref>

===1994–2012===
On June 3, 1994, in Brussels, the Zambian football team succumbed to one of the worst losses in its history against Belgium, losing 9–0. At [[1996 African Cup of Nations|CAN 1996]], they finished first in the group with two victories (5–1 against [[Burkina Faso national football team|Burkina Faso]], goals from [[Kenneth Malitoli]], double from [[Kalusha Bwalya]], goals from [[Dennis Lota]] and [[Johnson Bwalya]]); 4–0 against [[Sierra Leone national football team|Sierra Leone]] (a hattrick from [[Kalusha Bwalya]] and goal from [[Mordon Malitoli]]) and a draw (0–0 against [[Algeria national football team|Algeria]]), beat [[Egypt national football team|Egypt]] (3–1) in the quarterfinals and loses in semis against [[Tunisia national football team|Tunisia]] (2–4) but took third place over [[Ghana national football team|Ghana]] (1–0, goal from [[Johnson Bwalya]]). [[Kalusha Bwalya]] was the best in the competition with 5 goals. From 1998 to 2006, Zambia did not pass the first round, except in 2004 when they failed to qualify. On September 3, 2006, at home, Zambia achieved the biggest victory in its history against [[Djibouti national football team|Djibouti]], winning 10–0. During the [[2008 Africa Cup of Nations]], Zambia finished third in the group with a 3–0 victory against [[Sudan national football team|Sudan]] (goals by [[James Chamanga]], [[Jacob Mulenga]] and [[Felix Katongo]]), a draw (1–1 against [[Egypt national football team|Egypt]], goal from [[Chris Katongo]]) and loss (1–5 against [[Cameroon national football team|Cameroon]], goal from [[Chris Katongo]]). In 2010, Zambia finished first in its group and faced [[Nigeria national football team|Nigeria]] in the quarter-finals where they lost on penalties. [[Jacob Mulenga]] and [[Emmanuel Mbola]] were included in the tournament's Best XI.
In 2012, the best [[2012 Africa Cup of Nations|AFCON]] in Zambia history happened. During the tournament's group stage, they defeated [[Senegal national football team|Senegal]](2–1), drew with [[Libya national football team|Libya]] (2-2), and defeated [[Equatorial Guinea national football team|Equatorial Guinea]] (1–0), and qualified top of their group. During the knockout stage, Zambia defeated [[Sudan national football team|Sudan]] (3–0) in the [[Single-elimination tournament|Quarterfinals]], beat [[Ghana national football team|Ghana]] in the semifinals (1–0) and went up against [[Ivory Coast national football team|Ivory Coast]] in the [[2012 Africa Cup of Nations Final|final]], where they won their first title, after defeating them in a dramatic [[Penalty shootout]] after a 0–0 draw after [[Overtime (association football)|extra time]].<ref>{{cite web |title=CAF releases Top XI of Orange CAN |url=http://www.cafonline.com/competition/african-cup-of-nations-angola_2010/news/4614-caf-releases-top-11-of-orange-can.html |website=CAF Online |access-date=18 October 2021 |archive-date=4 February 2010 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20100204125848/http://www.cafonline.com/competition/african-cup-of-nations-angola_2010/news/4614-caf-releases-top-11-of-orange-can.html |url-status=live }}</ref>

==Managers==

[[File:Aljosa Asanovic.png|thumb|200px|[[Aljoša Asanović]] coached the national football team of Zambia]]
[[File:Avram Grant 2012.jpg|thumb|200px|[[Avram Grant]] became the manager of the national football team of Zambia in 2022]]

:Caretaker managers are listed in ''italics''.
{{div col|colwidth=25em}}
*{{flagicon|ZAM|1964}} [[Ted Virba]] (1978)
*{{flagicon|ENG}} [[Brian Tiler]] (1978–80)
*{{flagicon|ROM|1965}} [[Ted Dumitru]] (1980–81)
*{{flagicon|YUG}} [[Ante Bušelić]] (1981–82)
*{{flagicon|ENG}} [[Bill McGarry]] (1982–83)
*{{flagicon|POL}} [[Wieslaw Grabowski]] (1983–84)
*{{flagicon|ENG}} [[Jeff Butler (football manager)|Jeff Butler]] (1984)
*{{flagicon|ZAM|1964}} [[Roy Mulenga]] (1984)
*{{flagicon|ZAM|1964}} [[Brightwell Banda]] (1984–86)
*{{flagicon|ZAM|1964}} [[Samuel Ndhlovu]] (1987–92)
*{{flagicon|ZAM|1964}} [[Moses Simwala]] (1993)
*{{flagicon|ZAM|1964}} [[Godfrey Chitalu]] (1993)
*{{flagicon|DEN}} [[Roald Poulsen]] (1993–94)
*{{flagicon|SCO}} [[Ian Porterfield]] (1994)
*{{flagicon|DEN|1964}} [[Roald Poulsen]] (1994–96)
*{{flagicon|ZAM}} [[Freddie Mwila]] (1996–97)
*{{flagicon|ZAM}} ''[[George Mungwa]]'' (1997)
*{{flagicon|GER}} [[Burkhard Ziese]] (1997–98)
*{{Flagicon|ZAM}} ''[[George Mungwa]]'' (1998)
*{{flagicon|ZAM}} ''[[Obby Kapita]]'' (1998)
*{{flagicon|ZAM}} [[Fighton Simukonda]] (1998)
*{{flagicon|ZAM}} [[Ben Bamfuchile]] (1999–2000)
*{{flagicon|ZAM}} ''[[George Mungwa]]'' (2000)
*{{flagicon|NED}} [[Jan Brouwer]] (2000–01)
*{{flagicon|DEN}} [[Roald Poulsen]] (2002)
*{{flagicon|ZAM}} [[Patrick Phiri]] (2002–03)
*{{flagicon|ZAM}} [[Kalusha Bwalya]] (2003–06)
*{{flagicon|ZAM}} [[Patrick Phiri]] (2006–08)
*{{flagicon|FRA|1974}} [[Hervé Renard]] (2008–10)
*{{flagicon|ZAM}} [[Wedson Nyirenda]] & {{flagicon|ZAM}} [[Honour Janza]] (2010)
*{{flagicon|ZAM}} [[George Lwandamina]] (2010)
*{{flagicon|ITA}} [[Dario Bonetti]] (2010–11)
*{{flagicon|FRA|1974}} [[Hervé Renard]] (2011–13)
*{{flagicon|FRA|1974}} [[Patrice Beaumelle]] (2013–14)
*{{flagicon|ZAM}} [[Honour Janza]] (2014–15)
*{{flagicon|ZAM}} [[George Lwandamina]] (2015–16)
*{{flagicon|ZAM}} [[Wedson Nyirenda]] (2016–18)
*{{flagicon|ZAM}} [[Beston Chambeshi]] (2018)
*{{flagicon|BEL}} [[Sven Vandenbroeck]] (2018–19)
*{{flagicon|ZAM}} [[Aggrey Chiyangi]] (2019–20)
*{{flagicon|SRB}} [[Milutin Sredojević]]  (2020–21)
*{{flagicon|ZAM}} [[Beston Chambeshi]]  (2021–22)
*{{flagicon|CRO}} [[Aljoša Asanović]] (2022)
*{{flagicon|ZAM}} ''[[Moses Sichone]]'' (2022)
*{{flagicon|ISR}} [[Avram Grant]] (2022–2025)
*{{flagicon|ZAM}}''[[Moses Sichone]]'' (2025-present){{div col end}}

==Players==
===Current squad===
The following players were called up for the [[Exhibition game#International football|friendly match]] against [[Argentina national football team|Argentina]] on 31 March 2026.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://web.facebook.com/FootballAssociationZambia/posts/pfbid02gog4mGRKntXAhogQGQ1nTQoDvS869ryqSTuQwDkoRuXaWkBLhxKyCNNsGB9DKxPVl|title=FIFA WINDOW: Lwandamina Names 23 Bullets for the International Friendly against Argentina|publisher=[[Football Association of Zambia]]|access-date=23 March 2026|date=22 March 2026|via=Facebook}}</ref>

''Caps and goals as of 29 December 2025, after the game against [[Morocco national football team|Morocco]].''

{{nat fs break}}
;Notes
*<sup>DEC</sup> Refused to join the team after the call-up.
*<sup>INJ</sup> Withdrew due to an injury.
*<sup>PRE</sup> Preliminary Squad.
*<sup>RET</sup> Retired from international association football.
*<sup>SUS</sup> Suspended from the team.
{{nat fs end}}

==Player records==
{{Updated|31 March 2026}}<ref>{{cite web |url=https://www.rsssf.org/miscellaneous/zam-recintlp.html |title=Zambia&nbsp;– Record International Players |author=Jerry Muchimba and Roberto Mamrud |publisher=Rec.Sport.Soccer Statistics Foundation |access-date=1 January 2016 |archive-date=10 July 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220710163212/https://www.rsssf.org/miscellaneous/zam-recintlp.html |url-status=live }}</ref>
:''Players in '''bold''' are still active with Zambia.''

|-

|-

==Honours==
===Continental===
* '''[[Africa Cup of Nations]]'''
** {{Gold1}} '''Champions (1):''' [[2012 African Cup of Nations|2012]]
** {{Silver2}} Runners-up (2): [[1974 African Cup of Nations|1974]], [[1994 African Cup of Nations|1994]]
** {{Bronze3}} Third place (3): [[1982 African Cup of Nations|1982]], [[1990 African Cup of Nations|1990]], [[1996 African Cup of Nations|1996]]
* '''[[African Nations Championship]]'''
** {{Bronze3}} Third place (1): [[2009 African Nations Championship|2009]]

===Regional===
* '''[[COSAFA Cup]]'''
** {{Gold1}} '''Champions (7):''' [[1997 COSAFA Cup|1997]], [[1998 COSAFA Cup|1998]], [[2006 COSAFA Cup|2006]], [[2013 COSAFA Cup|2013]], [[2019 COSAFA Cup|2019]], [[2022 COSAFA Cup|2022]], [[2023 COSAFA Cup|2023]]
** {{Silver2}} Runners-up (6): [[2004 COSAFA Cup|2004]], [[2005 COSAFA Cup|2005]], [[2007 COSAFA Cup|2007]], [[2009 COSAFA Cup|2009]], [[2017 COSAFA Cup|2017]], [[2018 COSAFA Cup|2018]]
** {{Bronze3}} Third place (1): [[2008 COSAFA Cup|2008]]
* '''[[CECAFA Cup]]'''
** {{Gold1}} '''Champions (2):''' [[1984 CECAFA Cup|1984]], [[1991 CECAFA Cup|1991]]
** {{Silver2}} Runners-up (5): [[1976 CECAFA Cup|1976]], [[1977 CECAFA Cup|1977]], [[1978 CECAFA Cup|1978]], [[1988 CECAFA Cup|1988]], [[2006 CECAFA Cup|2006]]
** {{Bronze3}} Third place (5): [[1973 CECAFA Cup|1973]]{{sup|s}}, [[1974 CECAFA Cup|1974]]{{sup|s}}, [[1981 CECAFA Cup|1981]], [[1992 CECAFA Cup|1992]], [[2013 CECAFA Cup|2013]]

===Awards===
* '''[[CAF Awards#African National Team of the Year|African National Team of the Year]] (1)''': 2012
